was an American recording studio located in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California. During the 1940s and 1950s, Radio Recorders was one of the largest independent recording studios in the world. Notable musicians who recorded at Radio Recorders include [Billie Holiday](/wiki/Billie_Holiday "Billie Holiday"), [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker"), [Sam Cooke](/wiki/Sam_Cooke "Sam Cooke"), [Jimmie Rodgers](/wiki/Jimmie_Rodgers_(pop_singer) "Jimmie Rodgers (pop singer)"), [Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong"), [Mario Lanza](/wiki/Mario_Lanza "Mario Lanza"), [Patti Page](/wiki/Patti_Page "Patti Page"), [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix"), [Frankie Yankovic](/wiki/Frankie_Yankovic "Frankie Yankovic"), [Frank Zappa](/wiki/Frank_Zappa "Frank Zappa"), [Paul Frees](/wiki/Paul_Frees "Paul Frees") and [the Carpenters](/wiki/The_Carpenters "The Carpenters") among others. In its prime, the studio was considered the best recording facility in Los Angeles, with two large studios and some smaller ones, as well as disc mastering facilities.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[Roy, James](/w/index.php?title=James_V._Roy&action=edit&redlink=1 "James V. Roy (page does not exist)") (March 2004), [*Radio Recorders*](http://www.scottymoore.net/studio_radiorecorders.html), retrieved 2008-01-19
Moore, Scotty. ["Radio Recorders"](http://www.scottymoore.net/studio_radiorecorders.html). *scottymoore.net*. Scotty Moore. Retrieved 9 September 2021.

For the Russian radio station, see [Radio Record](/wiki/Radio_Record "Radio Record"). For the Brazilian radio station, see [Rádio Record](/wiki/R%C3%A1dio_Record "Rádio Record").

**Radio Recorders, Inc.** was an American recording studio located in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California. During the 1940s and 1950s, Radio Recorders was one of the largest independent recording studios in the world. Notable musicians who recorded at Radio Recorders include [Billie Holiday](/wiki/Billie_Holiday "Billie Holiday"), [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker"), [Sam Cooke](/wiki/Sam_Cooke "Sam Cooke"), [Jimmie Rodgers](/wiki/Jimmie_Rodgers_(pop_singer) "Jimmie Rodgers (pop singer)"), [Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong"), [Mario Lanza](/wiki/Mario_Lanza "Mario Lanza"), [Patti Page](/wiki/Patti_Page "Patti Page"), [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix"), [Frankie Yankovic](/wiki/Frankie_Yankovic "Frankie Yankovic"), [Frank Zappa](/wiki/Frank_Zappa "Frank Zappa"), [Paul Frees](/wiki/Paul_Frees "Paul Frees") and [the Carpenters](/wiki/The_Carpenters "The Carpenters") among others. In its prime, the studio was considered the best recording facility in Los Angeles, with two large studios and some smaller ones, as well as disc mastering facilities.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_Recorders&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Radio Recorders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_Recorders&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Radio Recorders")]

Originally founded in 1933 at 932 N. [Western Avenue](/wiki/Western_Avenue_(Los_Angeles) "Western Avenue (Los Angeles)"), Radio Recorders established itself at 7000 [Santa Monica Boulevard](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Boulevard "Santa Monica Boulevard") by 1949. During the 1940s and 1950s, Radio Recorders was responsible for recording [transcriptions](/wiki/Electrical_transcription "Electrical transcription") of countless radio shows, both network and local, for [delayed broadcast](/wiki/Broadcast_delay "Broadcast delay") in the [Western states](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States"). Since this was prior to [magnetic tape recording](/wiki/Magnetic_tape_recording "Magnetic tape recording"), the recordings were made on and played back from lacquer-coated [aluminum discs](/wiki/Aluminum_disc "Aluminum disc"). Studio C was Radio Recorders nerve center with at least six [recording lathes](/wiki/Disc_cutting_lathe "Disc cutting lathe") and turntables and an "on-the-air" playback turntable protected by a railing so that it would not be bumped while it was replaying a program on-air. The recording lathes were [shock-mounted in sand](/wiki/Vibration_isolation "Vibration isolation") to prevent rumble from the [streetcars](/wiki/Streetcars "Streetcars") on Santa Monica Boulevard. Studio C could handle several programs at once, 24 hours a day, and often with a single engineer on duty. Telephone lines ran to all the important stations and the networks.

Most of the major labels used Radio Recorders well into the 1960s. [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor"), [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records"), [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records"), and [Decca](/wiki/Decca_Records "Decca Records") utilized Radio Recorders prior to building their own West Coast facilities, and remained the choice of many independent labels and both popular and classical artists, from [Stravinsky](/wiki/Stravinsky "Stravinsky") to Elvis.

Radio Recorders was also where most commercial [jingles](/wiki/Jingle "Jingle") were recorded, and their dominance of this market segment strengthened in 1959 when their affiliate, MP-TV Services, acquired the rights to the sound effect, jingle and commercial aid libraries of Standard Radio Transcription, Inc.

In 1960, Radio Recorders merged with Universal Recorders, located at 6757 [Hollywood Blvd.](/wiki/Hollywood_Blvd. "Hollywood Blvd.") and built a new studio at 1441 North McCadden Place, which they called their Sunset-Highland Division. Radio-Universal Recorders was the largest recording studio in the United States.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Radio Recorders Annex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_Recorders&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Radio Recorders Annex")]

In 1946, Radio Recorders remodeled a former RCA Victor warehouse located at 1032 North Sycamore Avenue into a large studio capable of handling approximately fifty musicians. This studio was known as **Radio Recorders Annex**, or, within the industry, just "The Annex," after a legendary studio that Victor had built in the warehouse back in the 1930s.

In 1962 H.B. Barnum and Bill Aken chose The Annex to record their big band version of "Goody, Goody" for Governor Goodwin J. (Goodie) Knight's re-election campaign. The same year, Bill Aken recorded the classic "Theme For Shock Theater" with engineer Phil Yeend.

In 1965, Radio Recorders' [engineer](/wiki/Audio_engineering "Audio engineering"), [Thorne Nogar](/w/index.php?title=Thorne_Nogar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Thorne Nogar (page does not exist)"), purchased The Annex and started his own independent **Annex Studios**, which attracted a distinguished clientele as a full-service studio, including mastering. For many years, [Lawrence Welk](/wiki/Lawrence_Welk "Lawrence Welk") pre-recorded the music for his [popular television show](/wiki/The_Lawrence_Welk_Show "The Lawrence Welk Show"), becoming the most important client; in addition, in the 1960s and 1970s Annex mastered for such labels as [Uni Records](/wiki/Uni_Records "Uni Records"), [GNP Crescendo Records](/wiki/GNP_Crescendo_Records "GNP Crescendo Records"), [Dot Records](/wiki/Dot_Records "Dot Records"), and [Ranwood Records](/wiki/Ranwood_Records "Ranwood Records"), as well as cutting early pressings of [Barbra Streisand](/wiki/Barbra_Streisand "Barbra Streisand")'s hit single "[The Way We Were](/wiki/The_Way_We_Were_(song) "The Way We Were (song)")."

In January 1986, [Record Plant](/wiki/Record_Plant_West "Record Plant West") opened at the Annex location.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Studio 56

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_Recorders&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Studio 56")]

Main article: [Studio 56](/wiki/Studio_56 "Studio 56")

In the late 1980s the studio was reopened by Paul Schwartz as [Studio 56](/wiki/Studio_56 "Studio 56"). At that time, artists such as Guns & Roses, Sugar Ray, Toni Braxton, Brandy, No Doubt and others recorded there. The documentary *Standing in the Shadows of Motown* was also partially filmed there. Kenneth Crouch, Keith Crouch and Leon Sylvers were all house producers there at that time.

In 2002, the studio gained two new partners, Pride Hutchison and Michael Dumas who brought the studio back to life under the original Radio Recorders name, and in 2004, the city of Los Angeles installed a street sign describing the historical significance of the site.

From 2002 to 2008 the studio welcomed many great projects and clients including Natalie Cole, Dwight Yoakam, Lucinda Williams, Lil' Jon, Xzibit, Annie Lennox and many more.

The facility closed in 2008. Today the sign describing the historical significance of Radio Recorders still remains on the corner of Santa Monica and Orange.
